entry.title: Discovering Business Events from Lobby for SAP Build Process Automation : Manage Credit Memo Request
entry.content:
Introduction....

SAP Build Process Automation (SBPA) enables business users to automate workflow processes and tasks without writing code.

Check here for What's New and Learn more from the roadmap.  

This August 2024, SAP Build Process Automation has a released a new way of discovering Business Events from Lobby.

It enables users to discover events from Business Accelerator hub and to view the detailed event catalogue. It also facilitates reuse by creating centrally managed Events projects at Lobby level. Additionally using the Manual Event Specification Upload feature, all the SAP and non-SAP business events supporting CloudEvent1.0 format are supported which can be configured with Event Mesh(or Advanced Event Mesh) at runtime.

This blog will demonstrate the latest features of event triggers in the context of a SAP S/4HANA business use case:

  1. Monitoring Business Events tile
  2. Creating Event Projects from Lobby and consuming the events as event triggers in SAP Build Process Automation
  3. Run Now feature

The business use case explained….

Credit Memo Requests in SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud 

A credit memo request is a sales document used in complaints processing to request credit for a customer. Creating credit memo requests is a part of Sales and Distribution in SAP S/4HANA.  

In situations where the calculated prices are incorrect, or if discounts are not applied etc. a credit memo request can be created. The credit memo request can be automatically blocked for credit checking. Once it has been approved, you can remove the block. 

The system uses the credit memo request to create a credit memo. 

The SBPA Process for Manage Credit Memo Requests enables the business user to view the Credit Memo Request which are configured for external approvals and make a decision to Approve or Reject the Credit Memo Request which in turn will Release or Reject it in SAP S/4HANA or SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

 

Before we begin….

  • Credit Memo Requests are created in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud 
  • Credit Memo Request lifecycle events are pushed from SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud to Business Technology Platform through the Event Mesh configuration. Follow the blog  Event Mesh and SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Setup for SAP Build Process Automation for details on the setup to be done.

 

Creating Event Projects From Lobby….

From the SAP Build Lobby, click on Events and click Create . 

 

There are two options - One to browse SAP Business Accelerator Hub to discover public business events or to upload an event specification file. Choose SAP Business Accelerator Hub for this particular use case.

Search with Credit Memo and Select Credit Memo Request Events SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition for Business Events

All the available Credit Memo Request Events are displayed with relevant details. Click Add.

The Project Name and Description is automatically populated. Click Create to create the event project.

The Event Project is now created. Click Release to release the project.

 

Choose the required option and click Release.

The Event Project is now released. Click Publish to publish the project to Library. Once the event project is released to Library it can be consumed in SAP Build Process Automation(SBPA).

Click Publish.

Now the Event Project is ready to be consumed in the SAP Build Process Automation(SBPA) process.

Adding the event trigger….

The events available in the events project released in the above step are available as a part of the Library and can be used in the SAP Build Process Automation(SBPA).

For example, when you add an Event Trigger to the process , it automatically shows the list of events available.

In the SAP Build Process Automation(SBPA) process, click on Add a Trigger.

Click on Wait for an Event.

Select CreditMemoRequest Created event and click on Add.

 

Note that all the required details are filled, click on Create.

 

The event trigger is added to the process.

 

Designing the process….

A sample business process for Manage Credit Memo Requests could look like the following:

 

 

  • An event trigger is used to start the business process. The event used here is CreditMemoRequestCreated Event.
  • A further API Call is made to read the header information of the Credit Memo Request. The Credit Memo Request ID from the previous step is used as input for this API Call. The API in this example is
  • GET /A_CreditMemoRequest('{CreditMemoRequest}')
  • A set of business rules define the approver for the Credit Memo Request approval. The output is an approver email ID.
  • The approver determined from the previous step gets an Approval Task to release the Credit Memo Request in My Inbox with details of header and items and an option to Approve or Reject the request.
  • If the approver approves the credit memo request, quotes are added to the Credit Memo Request ID and the Credit Memo Request is released for further processing in the SAP S/4HANA system. The approval status will show ‘Released’ as the status of the Credit Memo Request.  An approval notification is sent to the stakeholders.
  • The API used in this example is POST /releaseApprovalRequest
  • If the approver rejects the credit memo request, quotes are added to the Credit Memo Request ID and the Credit Memo Request Approval Status is updated as Rejected in SAP S/4HANA system. A rejection notification is sent to the stakeholders.
  • The API used in this example is POST /rejectApprovalRequest

Now our SBPA process is ready.

Expert Tip :  Ensure to test your Actions projects and Events setup before testing 
the process to ensure they are working as expected. 
This will save time for troubleshooting and debugging later.

 

End-to-end execution….

  • When a credit memo request is created in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and is configured for external approval, then it automatically triggers the defined process in SAP Build Process Automation.

 

  • Just for confirmation, check the Approval Status in the SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud

 

  • Check if you received the event in Monitoring -> Acquired Events -> Business Events Tile. The events are persisted for 7 days and can be downloaded. Also they are very helpful in troubleshooting to understand if the events are correctly being received by SAP Build Process Automation.

 

 

  • You should get a task in My Inbox to Approve the Credit Memo Request

 

Add some comments and click approve.

When a Credit Memo Request is approved, it is released for further processing in the S/4HANA Public Cloud. The approval status will show ‘Released’ as the status of the Credit Memo Request   

 

OR  Add some comments and click Reject. Check that the S/4HANA Approval Status is updated as Rejected.

You can also check the SBPA Logs to check the details.

 

Run Now….

You can now test your processes locally that use event triggers by simulating the event trigger payload without connecting to the system or having an event mesh setup. This is to test if your end-to-end process works fine with the expected payload format for

“Run Now” action for event triggers in Trigger view allows to execute the event trigger in order to initiate event delivery with the user-provided data immediately. For more information, please refer to documentation here.

Below is  sample screenshot from Control Tower-> Environments -> Select your environment -> Triggers tab -> Select the required Event Trigger -> Run Now.

Enter a valid payload in the Run Now UI and Click Run Now.

 

The process is triggered with the sample payload.

 Note: The Manage Credit Memo Requests SBPA Process neither replaces nor has all the features of SAP Credit Management or Financial Supply Chain Management. It enables an approval process with information about the credit memo request. Our customers could use and extend this process for exception management, notifications, auditing for the particular credit memo requests after approval from one or more personas like credit manager, finance analysts, etc. 

Other use cases….

There are some additional use cases for business event triggers:

Business Event Triggers in SAP Build Process Automation for SAP S/4HANA Cloud : The Customer Return ...

Business Event Triggers in SAP Build Process Automation for SAP S/4HANA: Manage Credit Block on Sale...

Business Event Triggers in SAP Build Process Automation for SAP S/4HANA: Manage Billing Block on Sal...

Do write to me in case of blockers, queries and feedback.

entry.title: Help! Why Don’t I Get a Notification for My Custom Situation?
entry.content:
You’ve configured your own situation use case using the Extended Framework for Situation Handling. Now you’re waiting for a notification but nothing happens. What could have gone wrong?

Let me ask you a few questions to check whether your configuration is correct.

  • Has a situation occurred?
  • How have the conditions been set up?
  • Are all situation triggers covered?
  • Is recipient determination working?
  • Have email notifications been set up correctly?

These questions can support you as an SAP S/4HANA Cloud or SAP S/4HANA customer.

 

Has a situation occurred?

This may seem trivial but depending on the conditions that have been defined for the situation type, a situation instance may not have been created. An easy way to find out is for the user is to check the My Situations – Extended app to see if there’s an instance that they should have been notified about. If the instance is not displayed in the app, continue checking the conditions. If there’s an instance but no notification was sent, check the notifications / life cycle settings.

List of situaitons in My Situations – Extended app

Or, as a key user, you can display the current situation instances for a situation type by choosing Manage Instances in the header of the Manage Situation Types – Extended app.

Manage Instances in Manage Situation Types - Extended app

In the list of situation instances you can see if a notification was sent and when. Depending on the configuration, a notification can be sent only when a situation occurs for the first time or each time the situation is updated. If you don’t see an entry here, no notification was sent.

List of situation instances in Manage Situation Types - Extended app

There’s also the Monitor Situations – Extended app for key users that shows all created situation instances and all activities for an instance. You need to have situation monitoring enabled for the situation type in the Situation Instances section.

Enable the monitoring in Manage Situations - Extended app

List of situation instances in the Monitor Situations - Extended app

If no situation instance is displayed in the Manage Situation Types – Extended app and in the Monitor Situations – Extended app, this means that the users did not receive a notification because there no situation instance was created.  

If you expected a situation based on the conditions that you have defined, you may find clues in the following section on conditions.

If a situation instance is displayed but no notification was sent, check the notifications / life cycle setting.

How have the conditions been set up?

In the Conditions section of the Manage Situation Types – Extended app, let’s look at the settings for Notifications / Life Cycle, the Order of the conditions, and the Filters.

Notifications / life cycle settings

Each condition can have a different Notifications/Life Cycle setting. Make sure you’ve assigned the appropriate ones.

Notification setting

You can select from three standard options:

  1. The notification is sent to all recipients when a situation is created. When the situation is updated, only the assigned processors are notified.
  2. No one receives a notification after a situation is closed.
  3. All recipients receive a notification after a closed situation has been reopened (this option is available only for object-based situations with the instance closing behavior Close & Keep)

If you’ve defined your own Notification/Life Cycle behavior, I recommend double-checking the life cycle transitions. Make sure you have considered all relevant transitions from creation to closure. And check the notification settings for each transition.

Consider the processing order of conditions

If you’ve set up multiple conditions, the order of those conditions is important. The order defines the sequence in which the conditions are executed. It’s important to understand that the processing of the conditions stops when the first condition in the processing order is met. Conditions lower in the sequence are ignored. For this reason, you need to ensure that the more specific conditions, for which you want to have notifications, are being processed before the more generic conditions. Pay special attention to conditions that do not send notifications and those that close instances. If they are too high up in the sequence other conditions may always be ignored.

Processing order of conditions

Condition filters

Effective filters need to be of the same data format as the values in the CDS fields, such as strings or integers. You can find the reference to your object’s CDS views in the Manage Situation Objects app.

Sometimes the conditions and their filter values have been set up in such a way that no instances are created. Check whether one of the following reasons has prevented the creation of a situation instance:

  • The condition attribute filters are too constrained for your use case.
  • The attribute filter values overlap.
  • The attribute filter values are contradictory.

The condition and filter settings depend on your use case and the situation template on which your situation type is based. If you use multiple conditions, you can easily compare filters using the Compare Filters function.

Compare condition filters

Compare condition filters used

You can find some examples of using multiple conditions with filters in the blog posts Custom Situation Cases: Configure an Escalation Case and Custom Situation Cases: Configure a Complex Case.

 

Are all situation triggers covered?

Complex use cases often require multiple situation triggers. Check whether you have configured all possible triggers. And ensure that all triggers are enabled. There are two kinds of triggers: batch-based and event-based triggers.

Batch-based triggers periodically check the data you have defined in the condition filters.

  • The triggering object can be the anchor object or the trigger object.
  • The behavior of the trigger object is always Add/Update because the object still exists.

Use the Trigger Batch function to test immediately whether the situation trigger works as expected. Also check batch scheduling to make sure it’s configured correctly.

Batch-based situation triggers

Event-based triggers are based on an object’s status update. You can use multiple event-based triggers where you need to consider three aspects:

  • The triggering object: Multiple objects may impact a situation.
  • The trigger events such as create, update, and remove: Multiple events may impact a situation for a specific object.
  • The behavior of the trigger object can be Add/Update, Remove (Trigger data available), or Remove (Trigger data not available)

Event-based situation triggers

And these three aspects need to go hand in hand.

Situation objects that are created always have the trigger object behavior Add/Update because the object is added and exists.

The events that change an object can be manifold, for example, updated, confirmed, rejected, in progress, for approval, completed, released, posted, published, started, activated, blocked, unlocked, and many more. If the object still exists after the change, the trigger object behavior is Add/Update. If the object is flagged as removed or deleted, the trigger object behavior is Remove (Trigger data available). 

Objects that are deleted always have the trigger object behavior Remove (trigger data not available). This means the object is physically gone along with all its data. No more references are possible.

You can find an example for multiple triggers in the blog post Custom Situation Cases: Configure a Complex Case.

Should a situation trigger be missing in the situation type, you have the following options in the test system:

  1. Add a situation trigger from the value help in the situation type.
  2. If no appropriate situation trigger is available for the situation type, you can add a situation trigger with the suitable event to your situation scenario (Manage Situation Scenarios app). To do this you need to have the extensibility specialist role. Then proceed with (1).  Optionally, you can add the situation trigger to the related template so that it’s automatically available for all situation types created from the situation template.
  3. If no matching situation trigger is available in the situation scenario, you can enhance or add a situation object with the missing situation trigger (Manage Situation Objects app). Again, you need the extensibility specialist role. Then proceed with (2) and (1).

Afterwards, transport your situation type, situation scenario, and situation object to the productive system.

 

Is recipient determination working?

Situation Handling uses Responsibility Management to determine the users that are responsible for a situation. In the My Situations – Extended app, users see the created situations, and, depending on the notification settings, receive notifications.

When you create a situation type in the Recipients section, there are two basic options for defining these users:

  • Responsibility by Teams, which determines the users with the help of a team category.
  • Responsibility Rules, which determines the users with a BAdI business logic.

Situation recipients

Responsibility by Teams

To determine the right users by teams, a team category needs to be in place. The team category links Situation Handling and Responsibility Management. For a team category, at least one team needs to be enabled so that the right users can be detected. You enable teams in the Manage Teams and Responsibilities app. Also check whether all enabled teams have team members assigned to them and include the users who have to be informed about the situation.

Situation recipients by teams

You can add team members by selecting users from the value help. If you can’t find the user in the value help, check whether this user is registered as a business partner. If the business partner registration is missing, contact your system administrator to have your users registered.

If you use filters for responsibility definition or member functions in your situation type, make sure they are appropriate and don’t exclude your users. For detailed information about these filters, see the blog post Using Responsibility Teams in Situation Handling.

Responsibility Rules

Responsibility rules use the logic of a BAdI to determine the right users. If the identification of users isn’t working as expected, the logic of the BAdI can be the reason why your users were not found and therefore did not get any notifications.

Situation recipients by rules

If your situation type uses a standard responsibility rule, please open a ticket on component CA-GTF-RM to have the rule checked.

For your custom implementation, you can check the BAdI implementation logic yourself. (You’ll need the SAP_BR_EXTENSIBILITY_SPEC role to do that). Then proceed as follows:

  • In the Manage Responsibility Context app, on the Extended tab, search for the parent responsibility context to find your extended responsibility context.
  • Open the extended responsibility context to display the details. Here, you can see the responsibility rule ID associated with the responsibility rule that is displayed in the situation type.
  • In the Custom Logic app, you need to filter the list for the responsibility rule business context. Then, search for the static filter value that is identical to the responsibility rule ID. In the details, you can then check the BAdI implementation logic.

For detailed information about custom responsibility rules, you can refer to the blog post Responsibility Management: Create Your Rules. The blog post Using Responsibility Rules in Situation Handling illustrates how responsibility rules and Situation Handling work hand in hand.

 

Have email notifications been set up correctly?

In addition to the SAP Fiori launchpad notifications, users can opt to receive email notifications for each situation type in their SAP Fiori launchpad settings. For details, see Enable Email Notifications for End Users.

With SAP S/4HANA Cloud 2408 email notifications are enabled automatically. In older releases users need to enable email notifications in their SAP Fiori launchpad settings. To be able to activate this option, users need to have received an SAP Fiori launchpad notification before they can see and select the situation type in the launchpad settings.

Notification settings

Users may also want to check the launchpad settings to see whether the email address for their user account is correct:

Email settings

If all the settings are correct and you're still not getting any emails, you may want to check whether the email domain is on the list of allowed domains. The list of sender domains is maintained in Output Management. You can find more information in the chapter Defining an Email Sender Domain in the Setup Guide for SAP Best Practices for Output Management.

For specific questions or issues with the notification framework, please open a ticket on component OPU-GW-NOT.

 

Additional prerequisites for SAP S/4HANA

As an SAP S/4HANA customer, you may need to check some additional settings for the Notification Channel Framework which are automatically in place for SAP S/4HANA Cloud customers. You can also refer to the blog post Enable Email Notification in Notification Channel Framework. If you want to enable email notifications per default, you can implement the SAP Note 3417113.

To be on the safe side, you may also want to check whether you have made the required technical configuration settings.

  • Set up technical jobs
  • Activate OData services for apps
  • Define authorization for apps (optional)
  • Activate OData services for APIs (optional)
  • Define authorizations for APIs (optional)

 

Did this help you?

If not → just open a ticket on our component CA-SIT-ENG and we’ll take care of your problem. In the ticket, please let us know the situation scenario and the situation template your situation type is based on. Please attach screenshots of your situation type’s Conditions and Recipients sections. Or tell us which of the steps above you’ve already tried.

 

For more information, see

SAP Help Portal for

  • Situation Handling in SAP S/4HANA Cloud
  • Situation Handling in SAP S/4HANA.

SAP Community

  • Intelligent Situation Handling

entry.title: Changes in the Communication of the Electronic Nota Fiscal for Electrical Energy
entry.content:
Versão em português

Hi everyone,

The SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, outbound invoicing option for Brazil team has released updates on the communication of the Electronic Nota Fiscal for Electrical Energy (NF3-e) solution with SEFAZ.

NF3-e Technical Note 2024.002 1.01 Version

This update complies with the SEFAZ NF3-e Technical Note 2024.002 1.01 Version, which announced the discontinuation of the asynchronous authorization service, also known as the batch submission service. The communication between the SAP service and SEFAZ now is synchronous.

This communication change has been updated in the outbound invoicing option for Brazil service, and it is not necessary to implement SAP Notes in your backend system. For official communication on this update, read the SAP Note 3509349.

Check out the latest updates and features in our What's New session. And for a step-by-step guide on how to use the Electronic Nota Fiscal for Electrical Energy (NF3-e) solution, refer to the following SAP Help Portal pages:

  • Integration > NF3-e Specifics
  • Using Outbound Nota Fiscal > NF3-e Specifics

Related blog posts:

  • Usage Analytics Dashboard
  • How to Easily Browse the SAP Help Portal Pages for Outbound Nota Fiscal
  • An Introduction to SAP Document and Reporting Compliance: Navigating the SAP Help Portal

You can sign up for email alerts when new SAP Notes are released or updated in the components listed below; just follow the instructions in SAP Knowledge Base Article 2478289.

  • CA-GTF-CSC-EDO-BR-OU (Electronic Nota Fiscal for Electrical Energy, NF3-e)
  • FI-LOC-NFE-BR-OUT (Nota Fiscal Eletrônica Outbound)

Use the regulatory change manager tool to find out if a solution to a legal change is planned. Take a look at SAP Knowledge Base Article 3421995 and the regulatory change manager page to understand how to use this tool.

Have you found this blog post useful? If so, choose “like” (kudos), share it with your colleagues, and follow me for similar content. You are welcome to leave your questions and read similar blog posts on the SAP Document and Reporting Compliance SAP Community. Don’t forget to follow the SAP Electronic Invoicing for Brazil (SAP Nota Fiscal Eletronica) tag and the SAP Document and Reporting Compliance community to keep up with the latest NF-e news.

Thank you, and until next time,
Thiago

Tags: brazil, brasil, einvoice, einvoicing, e-invoicing, electronic invoice, electronic invoicing, edocument, edoc, e-document, e-docs, e-doc, e-docs, drc, document compliance, nota fiscal eletrônica, electronic nota fiscal, outbound, nf3-e, nf-e, electric energy, energia elétrica, is-u, nota técnica 2024.002, 2024/002, 2024.002

entry.title: Mudanças na comunicação da nota fiscal eletrônica de energia elétrica
entry.content:
English version here

Olá pessoal,

O time de SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, opção de emissão de fatura de saída para o Brasil entregou atualizações na comunicação da solução de nota fiscal eletrônica de energia elétrica (NF3-e) com a SEFAZ.

NF3-e Nota Técnica 2024.002 Versão 1.01

 


Esta atualização está conforme a Nota Técnica 2024.002 versão 1.01 de NF3-e da SEFAZ, que anunciou a descontinuação do serviço de autorização assíncrona, também conhecido como serviço de envio em lote. A comunicação entre o serviço da SAP e a SEFAZ agora é síncrona.

Esta alteração de comunicação foi atualizada na opção de emissão de fatura de saída para o Brasil, e não é necessário implementar o SAP Notes no seu sistema backend. Para comunicação oficial sobre esta atualização, leia a SAP Note 3509349.

Confira as últimas atualizações e recursos na página Novidades do Nota fiscal de saída no portal SAP Help. Para obter um passo a passo sobre como usar a solução de nota fiscal eletrônica de energia elétrica (NF3-e), consulte as seguintes páginas do portal SAP Help:

  • Integração > Específicos da NF3-e
  • Utilizar a nota fiscal de saída > Específicos da NF3-e

Blog posts relacionados:

  • Dashboard do Usage Analytics
  • Como navegar facilmente nas páginas do SAP Help Portal para Outbound Nota Fiscal
  • Navegando no portal SAP Help (inglês)

Você pode receber alerta por email quando novas SAP Notes forem lançadas ou atualizadas nos componentes listados abaixo; basta seguir as instruções do SAP Knowledge Base Article 2171560.

  • CA-GTF-CSC-EDO-BR-OU (Nota fiscal eletrônica de energia elétrica, NF3-e)
  • FI-LOC-NFE-BR-OUT (Nota Fiscal Eletrônica Outbound)

Acesse o gerenciador de modificações legais para ter uma noção do que está planejado para ser suportado. Consulte o SAP Knowledge Base Article 3421995 e a página Regulatory Change Manager para entender como usar esta ferramenta.

Gostou desse post? Dê um “like” (kudos) e compartilhe o conteúdo com seus colegas. Sinta-se à vontade para deixar suas perguntas no SAP Community e ler posts semelhantes no blogging space do SAP Document and Reporting Compliance. Para acompanhar as últimas notícias da NF-e, siga a tag SAP Electronic Invoicing for Brazil (SAP Nota Fiscal Eletronica) e a comunidade SAP Document and Reporting Compliance.

Obrigado e até a próxima,
Thiago

Tags: brazil, brasil, einvoice, einvoicing, e-invoicing, electronic invoice, electronic invoicing, edocument, edoc, e-document, e-docs, e-doc, e-docs, drc, document compliance, nota fiscal eletrônica, electronic nota fiscal, outbound, nf3-e, nf-e, electric energy, energia elétrica, is-u, nota técnica 2024.002, 2024/002, 2024.002

entry.title: Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Clicking Through
entry.content:
Introduction

From 2402 Release, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition added a new system monitoring capability, collectively called the Technical Monitoring Cockpit for monitoring the ABAP system.  The Cockpit provides a seamless connection between your system workload and single ABAP statistics records (main records and sub-records) down to SQL statements and their prepared plan.  Now you have a good starting point to conduct performance analysis on the SAP S/4HANA system in case of high response times. 

As this Technical Monitoring Cockpit is not unique to the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, but also to the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and the Business Technology Platform, the discussion here applies to them as well.

I plan to tackle this topic in a series of blogs in bite sizes so that you can easily consume them during your day-to-day work.

  • Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Clicking Through: this blog provides a guided tour of the Cockpit to readers, walkthrough (clicking through) various functions and resolve some confusions.
  • Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – The Key Concepts: this blog explains what metrics we are looking for in the Cockpit, and how they can be helpful to you, especially when something seems suspicious. 
  • Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Diving into Your Own System (to be published):  this blog discusses how to analyze a situation unique to you, which might or might not be answered in the collected system data.

Let’s do a guided tour in this blog: What is the Technical Monitoring Cockpit, how to access it, and how to navigate different screens in the Cockpit.

 

What is the Technical Monitoring Cockpit?

First thing first, I want to solve a mystery around theTechnical Monitoring Cockpit at the very beginning!

When you get on an SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition system, search a Fiori application called Technical Monitoring Cockpit, you will find nothing. Wait a minute, when you search the same app in the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library, you easily find the app Technical Monitoring Cockpit with the App ID F4031.

The Technical Monitoring Cockpit App as Shown in the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library

In the Implementation Information section, it lists below App Launchers, i.e. these apps launch the Technical Monitoring Cockpit App:

  • HANA Thread Samples
  • SQL Trace Analysis
  • Sampled Work Process Data
  • System Outbound Communication
  • System Workload
  • Table Analysis

You might ask what is going on here? Here is the story: The Technical Monitoring Cockpit was designed as a big workbench tool for system root cause analysis. Under this umbrella tool, there are many Apps, like System Workload, HANA Thread Samples, System Outbound Communication, etc. as shown in the above list. You can view these apps as an entrance to the system analysis, and they are all under the Technical Monitoring Cockpit umbrella after getting into the door.  For example, if you search HANA Thread Samples App, it still displays Technical Monitoring Cockpit as the app title (shown below).  Only the chart section and the table section have the title of HANA Thread Samples.

 The HANA Thread Samples App

Now if you click on the Apps -> Recently Used (or Apps -> Most Used), the app HANA Thread Samples is listed as the Technical Monitoring Cockpit. When you click it, it goes back to the HANA Thread Samples App.

The Technical Monitoring Cockpit Is Shown as an App in the Recently Used or Most Used Tab

 

Also you will notice that HANA Thread Samples App doesn’t exist in the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library.  When you type the name to search it, it returns only the Technical Monitoring Cockpit.

Here is the conclusion:

  • There is no app called Technical Monitoring Cockpit in the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition system, although it is listed in the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library.   It is a system analysis workbench title.
  • To explore different features of the Technical Monitoring Cockpit, you need to enter the name of various analysis apps, such as HANA Thread Samples.
  • In the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library, you don’t find any system analysis app like HANA Thread Samples.  It points you to the Technical Monitoring Cockpit.

This confusion will be resolved by our development, but I don’t know when.

 

Accessing the Technical Monitoring Cockpit

To access the Technical Monitoring Cockpit, you need to have the right authorizations, especially the Business Catalogs.  The Business Catalogs grant the access to the Technical Monitoring Cockpit are:

Business Catalog

Business Catalog Description

SAP_A4C_BC_DEV_SUP_PC

Development - Analysis and Support

SAP_CORE_BC_TMC_MON_PC

Technical Monitoring – Analysis

In the Fiori Launchpad, we organize apps by Spaces and Pages.  The Pages list relevant apps are:

Page

Page Description

Page Title

SAP_A4C_PGT_DEV_SUP_PC

Development - Analysis and Support

Analysis and Support

SAP_CORE_PGT_TMC_PC

Administration - Technical Monitoring

Technical Monitoring

Here is the example of the Technical Monitoring page. The relevant apps are marked with a medal.

The Technical Monitoring Page

 

The Pages are organized under Spaces which in turn are assigned to a business role.  The following four Spaces contain above two Pages:

Space

Space Description

Space Title

SAP_BR_ADMINISTRATOR

Administrator

Administration

SAP_BR_APPL_SUP_ENG_DEV_SUP

Application Support Engineer - Development Supporter

Development Support

SAP_BR_DEVELOPER

Developer

Development

SAP_OC_SPT_ADMINISTRATION_PC

Administration - Output Control

Administration - Output Control

In my case, I access these apps by clicking on the Administration Space, then the Technical Monitoring Page on the Fiori Launchpad (see above figure).

 

Walking through the Technical Monitoring Cockpit

When you are in the Technical Monitoring Cockpit, regardless of how you enter there through which door/entrance/app (such as HANA Thread Samples), click on the so-called Hamburger Menu (next to SAP logo), a menu panel is opened on the lefthand side as shown.

 Menu Panel of the Technical Monitoring Cockpit

It is not obvious that these menus lead to the relevant apps.  For example, when you click on the menu Dashboards -> Application System Overview, you can see four apps are listed:

 Four Apps under Dashboard ->Application System Overview

On the other hand, when you click on the menu Information -> Workload -> Captured Request Statistics, the app Captured Request Statistics is launched directly.  You cannot drill down further by clicking on the arrow icon (details).

 The Captured Request Statistics App

I am listing these menus and their relevant Apps in the below table.  I add brief comments to some apps as I don’t plan to discuss further. All other apps will be explained in my blogs.

 

Navigating the Technical Monitoring Cockpit

Regardless which app you use, the navigation technique is basically the same by clicking and drilling down.  Let me use System Workload app as an example, as it has more data to work with.  I close the Hamburger Menu to save the limited launchpad space.

The System Workload app can be launched in three ways: 1) Using Hamburger Menu, Dashboards -> Application System Overview-> System Workload: Response Time Contribution.  2) Using App Finder and type in “System Workload”.  3) Using Space and Pages, Administration-> Technical Monitoring-> System Workload.

In the System Workload screen, there are four sections vertically: Title, Time Range, Chart and Details Table (Top 10 entries if there are more than ten).

The First Window of the System Workload App

 

Title Section

There is not much you can do in this section.

Time Range Section

To define a range of data you are going to focus on, you need to define a time range. The Time Slider is visual and convenient but not accurate. More accurate definition is by using the time windows on the left and right.  For example, currently I define a time range starting from 2024-09-16 11:40 to 2024:09:17 11:40.  That is exact one day of length.  This duration information is displayed in the center of the window as “1 D”. If we extend the time range, the duration could be “1D 1h 19m”.

The double arrow icon (left and right) helps you to move the Time Slider exactly to the left or right by the duration. If the duration is “1D 1h”, the new time range becomes 2024-09-15 10:40 to 2024:09:16 11:40.

There is a pull-down window to adjust the granularity of the data.  By default, it is “In 10-Minute Intervals”.  I change that to “In Hours” to be used in Section Chart discussion later.

Chart Section

Depending on what data you are looking at, it mostly shows the stacked bar chart or a line chart.

In addition, this chart has multiple tabs. When you click on one of them, it opens a similar screen with a chart and a table for that particular interested area, such as ABAP CPU Time. I will leave them for discussion in the subsequent blogs.  From chart navigation perspective, they all behave the same way.

To check out a specific bar, you can hoover the mouse over the bar. It shows the time and value of the data in a pop-up label.

 Hoover Over to Read a Bar

Another way to read the bar value is by clicking on that bar.  A vertical dash line appears on that time, and all the same type bars are highlighted.  The value of that bar you clicked is shown at the upper left.

 Click to Read a Bar Value and Time

If you click on the ABAP Processing Time icon in the Legend, it highlights all the same type bars and displays the Mean Value of that bar type.  In this case, it is 20.16 seconds.

Click the Legend Icon to Read a Mean Value of One Type of Bars

The top row of the Chart Section has many filters. In the Public Cloud, the Fiori apps use OData V2, so this is a good Request Entry Type to choose.  With this filter, the details table is impacted as well, by only showing the data associated with the OData V2 requests.

Filter Data with Request Entry Type = OData V2

At the table, each vertical column values can be filtered as well by double clicking the column title.  But this filter only affects the value display in the column.  The filter in the Chart Section affects both chart and table.

 

Details Table Section

The Details Table below the Chart usually lists top ten data records.  At the bottom, the R stands for Rest, i.e., the total amount minus the sum of top ten records.  The T stands for Total, i.e., the total amount of top ten records in the table.  If there are less than ten records in the table, the total value of R should be zero.  T will be whatever is available in the table. 

The primary function of the Details Table is to drill down by clicking on the Details Icon at the end of each row. 

Let’s take a closer look at the top row.  Its Request Entry Name is /SAP/ESH_SEARCH_SRV/0001, and the Program Context is /sap/opu/odata/sap/ESH_SEARCH_SRV/DataSources.  It tells us the top workload is caused by a search function originated from a Fiori app.  By clicking on the Details Icon, we can have further information about this search program: how it consumes ABAP Processing Time within this 24-hour period. 

Details of Request Processing Time

Here you need to be careful.  The chart uses a set of aggregated data. When you use the granularity of hours, you could find peak values totally different from using granularity of As Collected. 

The three figures below show the ABAP Processing Time in three granularities: As Collected, At 10-Minute Intervals, In Hours. The peak Processing Times shift based on the granularity: 4:49:00, 4:50:00, 7:00:00, respectively.  Obviously, the Peak Value at 7 am is quite misleading.

 ABAP Processing Time Peak at 4:49:00 (Granularity: As Collected)

 ABAP Processing Time Peak at 4:50:00 (Granularity: At 10-Minute Intervals)

 ABAP Processing Time Peak at 7:00:00 (Granularity: In Hours)

When you change the granularity, the table rows change as well.  The time is aggregated according to the granularity.  To get an accurate entry point for analysis, I would use the granularity of “As Collected”. 

The table is sorted by the column ABAP Processing Time, with the highest value at the top.  From the Start Time column, you can observe the top Processing Times, some are on Sep. 16, and some are in Sep. 17. 

Now if you drill down further of the top row, you will reach the ABAP Statistics Record window.  Immediately you learn that there are 12 records and this is the first one (1/12), the highest ABAP Processing Time. By clicking on the arrows at the top, you can scroll through these twelve rows.

ABAP Statistics Record Screen

When you look at the ABAP Statistics Record, not only you can find out who runs this program at that time, but also status of Database Connections and Memory Consumptions.  I will discuss them in subsequent blogs.

 

Conclusion

The Technical Monitoring Cockpit is an umbrella covering many analysis tools/apps for administrators and developers.  You can enter this Cockpit from different entrances/apps.  The layout and structure of different apps under this umbrella follow a systematic approach from the navigation perspective.  After reading this blog, you should be NEVER LOST when using this new tool.

 

 

References:

  • SAP Help: Monitoring the System Workload
  • Blog: Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Clicking Through
  • Blog: Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – The Key Concepts 
  • Blog: Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Diving into Your Own System Workload (to be published)

entry.title: Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Key Concepts
entry.content:
Introduction

From 2402 Release, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition added a new system monitoring capability, collectively called the Technical Monitoring Cockpit for monitoring the ABAP system.  The Cockpit provides a seamless connection between your system workload and single ABAP statistics records (main records and sub-records) down to SQL statements and their prepared plan.  Now you have a good starting point to conduct performance analysis on the SAP S/4HANA system in case of high response times. 

As this Technical Monitoring Cockpit is not unique to the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, but also to the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and the Business Technology Platform, the discussion here applies to them as well.

I plan to tackle this topic in a series of blogs in bite sizes so that you can easily consume them during your day-to-day work.

  • Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Clicking Through: this blog provides a guided tour of the Cockpit to readers, walkthrough (clicking through) various functions and resolve some confusions.
  • Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – The Key Concepts: this blog explains what metrics we are looking for in the Cockpit, and how they can be helpful to you, especially when something seems suspicious. 
  • Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Diving into Your Own System (to be published):  this blog discusses how to analyze a situation unique to you, which might or might not be answered in the collected system data.

Let’s talk about the Key Concepts in this blog: the Server Response Time, The ABAP Statistics Records, the Sampled Work Process Data and the Single Work Process Sample.

 

Server Response Time

In the SAP Help – Server Response Time Contribution, there is a good illustration of the time elements contributing to the total Server Response Time (see below).

 Elements of Server Response Time

The Server Response Time counts from a request is put into a queue for processing, until an answer returns to the queue for picking up. The time spent on Dispatcher/Gateway/Internet Communication Manager is not counted.  The large chunk of time is spent in ABAP Work Process and HANA Database.  The Database Network Time depends on how many data records are transmitted back to the ABAP Work Process; it is a significant portion of the Database Processing Time, as the in-memory data processing is much faster than transmitting data over a network.

 

ABAP Statistics Records or ABAP Request Statistics

In an ABAP environment, each running of an ABAP program is recorded with technical information, such as response time, program name, CPU time, etc.  This statistics record is written to the system.  With these records, an administrator or a developer can investigate the performance or resource consumption in detail, so that you can identify the cause of a problem should a performance problem occurs.

The use cases for ABAP Statistics Records are:

  • Top system workload consumers and their response times and database connections
  • A specific workload associated with a user or a program at a particular time

The below figure is a good illustration of ABAP Statistics Record and its screen, and how to get there: Information -> Workload -> Captured Request Statistics.

ABAP Statistics Record Screen

The ABAP Statistics Record screen includes a lot of information, namely

  • Server Response Time Contribution, including processing times, wait times, load times, database network time, and so on
  • Subsequent sub-records if they are available
    • SQL Statements
    • Database Connections
    • Extended Passport
    • Memory
    • Gateway Calls
    • Badi Calls
    • HTTP Client/Server Calls
    • RFC Client Calls
    • Web Service: Consumer Calls

How many sub-records are shown depends on the selected main record. For example, if a database request has been made in the context of the action for the main record, there's a sub-record for database connections.  Otherwise, there is no database connection sub-records.

 

How Are ABAP Statistics Records Collected?

In an ABAP system, when a HTTP call is made to the application server, it goes through the Internet Communication Manager (ICM), which places it in a queue.  When a Work Process becomes available, it picks up the request from the queue and process it.  At the end of processing, it writes a statistics record into the buffer. If the buffer is full, old records are written to the ABAP Statistics Files to make room for the new records.  This ABAP Statistics Records Collection process is shown below.

 ABAP Statistics Records Collection Process

It is important to keep below two points in mind when considering how often you get ABAP statistics records:

  1. Before ABAP statistics records are shown in the Technical Monitoring Cockpit, they are captured and processed by a collector that runs every minute, so you must expect that captured request statistics are displayed with a delay of a minute.
  2. If you have a long-running request, ABAP statistics records will only appear for this request after the request is done. The collector then captures the request and calculates its workload over its runtime.

ABAP statistics records are the data basis for system workload analysis, including the System Workload and the Captured Request Statistics in the Technical Monitoring Cockpit.  Aggregates are built using all records, but only samples of single records are stored and shown. In other words, one single statistics record for one specific program and one specific workload doesn’t exist. All the records contain aggregated data, even we call some of them “Single Records”.

 

ABAP Statistics Records Capture Profiles

Different from what you think, the ABAP Statistics Records are not a complete set of records for the selected period, rather, they are samples of them after aggregation, based on selection criteria defined in capture profiles.

The ABAP Statistics Records Capture Profiles are defined in the Capture Request Statistics app.  This app cannot be accessed within the Technical Monitoring Cockpit.  You have two options: 1) In the Fiori Launchpad, follow the Administrator Space to the Technical Monitoring Page.  2) Call the app directly from the App Finder.

Below figure shows the Capture Request Statistics app screen. It lists seven profiles, six of them are defined by SAP and you cannot change them.  One profile Z_GEORGE is user defined, by me.  The Profile Type indicates they are all for ABAP Statistics Records, while SAP profiles are forever active, my profile Z_GEORGE is currently Inactive. User can decide when and how long the user defined profile runs.

Capture Request Statistics App Screen

The first SAP profile is called SAP_DYNAMIC_CAPTURING. It captures “good” statistics samples of requests with respect to the server response time. As a result, you can compare statistics of samples of “bad” workload to statistics of samples of “good” workload. The top workload in your system might vary as well as the behavior in response times change over the time. The dynamic profile adapts to these changes.

In addition, for the top workload, the dynamic profile captures samples of ABAP statistics when their server response times deviate too much from the average server response time of this specific kind of workload. This ensures that for workloads with typically different server response times, samples of ABAP statistics records are also at hand, even if their server response times do not exceed the static thresholds of the static profile types.

The remaining five SAP profiles capture “bad” requests (high consumption of computation resources) in their respective areas: ABAP CPU time, ABAP processing time, HANA CPU time, HANA processing time, and Server response time.

To define your own Profile, click on Create button in the Capture Request Statistics app screen.  The Profile definition screen appears. Here you need to enter the following:

  • Profile ID: follow naming convention in the SAP Universe, avoid using “SAP” as prefix. Z and Y are a good choice.
  • Description: give a meaningful description
  • Profile Type: choose one from two: ABAP Statistics Records (Static) or SQL Trace
  • Target User Group: see below table

Target Group

Description

Customer Business

Only system activities of business users are captured

Customer Communication

Only system activities of communication users are captured

Customer Print

Only print requests are captured

Customer

System activities of all of the above target groups are captured

 

  • Retention Time: depending on your business needs, you can choose from 1 Hour, 1Day or 2 Weeks.
  • Record Limit per Minute (the maximum number of records you want the system to capture per minute): 1,000 is a good number. If the number is too high, it uses a lot of memory to store the data.  SAP profiles use 500.
  • Sampling Rate (the probability of capturing one single ABAP statistics record): 100 %
  • Health Monitoring: if clicked, the data is sent to the SAP Cloud Application Lifecycle Monitoring (Cloud ALM).

Capture ABAP Statistics Record App – Profile Definition Screen

You might ask what the difference between my profile and SAP’s profiles is. Well, it has a lot to do with how you define and control the profile to run.  After you hit the Create button in the Profile Definition screen, you get the 2nd screen Conditions for ABAP Statistics Records.

Capture ABAP Statistics Record App – Condition List Screen

When hit Create button in the Condition List Screen, you enter the Condition Creation/Edit Screen.  In the Field ID, you can choose one specific area you are interested in, say ABAP CPU Time [ms]. Next, you need to define what range of CPU Time you want to capture by using the Low/High values and an operator, such as “exclude less than 100”.  This condition will collect ABAP CPU Time Records larger and equal to 100 [ms].

Capture ABAP Statistics Record App – Condition Creation/Edit Screen

Here is the screenshot of the profile Z_GEORGE2 with its condition set. If you are ready, you can activate this profile to run in a specific amount of time.  You can see all the ABAP Statistics Records collected by following this profile when clicking Analyze Request Statistics button.  I will discuss this topic in my next blog Introduce Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Diving into Your Own System.

Capture ABAP Statistics Record App – A User Defined Profile with a Condition

 

Sampled Work Process Data

Besides ABAP Statistics Records, we have another type of statistics data called Sampled Work Process Data.  People are easy to get confused by two of them.

The Sampled Work Process Data focuses on monitoring work processes and concurrent workloads at a specific time.  It provides an overview of the workload on your ABAP system with the help of samples that are taken in a high frequency (every second).  Data are aggregated as well, including ABAP CPU time, main memory consumption, and work process utilization.

In the SAP Help, there is a good explanation on the difference between ABAP Statistics Records, Sampled Work Process Data and HANA Thread Samples. I use its figure and explanation here, except my notes in italic.

Explanation on the difference among ABAP Statistics Records, Sampled Work Process Data and HANA Thread Samples

You've got four ABAP workloads here. Each of them starts at a different point in time and runs for a certain time.

For the Sampled Work Process Data app, a sample is created with high frequency. For the sake of simplicity in this example, let's assume that a sample is taken every 20 seconds, at the times t1, t2, and so on. The samples taken at the time t2 and t3, for example, then captured data from ABAP Workload 1 and ABAP Workload 2 at exactly these two points in time. ABAP Workload 2 is also captured in the samples at the time t3 and t4. ABAP Workload 3 is included in the sample taken at the time t6 only. However, ABAP Workload 4 isn't part of any of these samples because it's running in the gap between the samples at the times of t2 and t3. About ABAP Workloads 1 and 2, you get information at multiple points in time, so you have details about the behavior of this workload over time, which might help you to identify resource consumption peaks.

In contrast, for the System Workload app, a collection of ABAP statistics records for completed workloads is scheduled once per minute. This regular data collection captures completed workload asynchronously. This means that ABAP Workload 1 to ABAP Workload 4 are captured and taken into account for the aggregated data shown in the System Workload app. At time t4, the ABAP statistics records for Workload 1 and Workload 4 are processed because they are completed. One minute later (three 20 sec intervals) at time t7, Workloads 2 and 3 are processed. (George’s Note: I changed last two sentences from SAP Help, because I believe my discussion is more accurate in explaining the ABAP Statistic Record collection mechanism.)

All in all, when you call up the System Workload app, you first get aggregates that are based on ABAP statistics records. These aggregates are always complete in the sense that they are based on all ABAP statistics records in the system. There are no gaps where an individual workload might slip through as it might happen with the collection of individual work process samples, such as Workload 4 in the example. With the collection of individual work process samples, you get an insight into a running workload, like for ABAP Workload 2 at t2, t3, t4, and t5. Therefore, the individual work process samples can give you more fine-granular details about the workload.

George’s addition/simplification: The ABAP Statistics Records collect the information contributed by the completed workloads not collected in the previous point (green box and arrows); In contrast, the Sampled Work Process Data show the information of one or many workloads running at one specific time point (red line and arrow).  They are just two different perspectives of the system performance to help you understand the system better: one look deeper at the running workloads and another one look back to those completed workloads.

Similar to work process samples, HANA thread samples are also collected every second and before the actual workload is completed. The HANA thread samples give you an idea of your HANA workload in the SAP HANA database server.

~From SAP Help

To view the ABAP Statistic Records, you use the System Workload app; to view the Sampled Work Process Data, you use the Sampled Work Process Data app. Both apps can be found in the App Finder, or the Fiori Launchpad -> Administration Space -> Technical Monitoring Page.

Below is the screenshot of the Sampled Work Process Data app.  In the app, you can explore four metrics over a period of time:

  • ABAP CPU Time
  • Main Memory
  • Dialog Work Process Utilization
  • Background Work Process Utilization

 Sampled Work Process Data app – ABAP CPU Time Screen

One concept associated with the Sampled Work Process Data is called “Request Entry Point”, a combination of Request Entry Type + Request Entry Name + Tenant ID.  For example, in the table (ABAP CPU Time Details) of above screenshot, we look at the first row of data:

  • Request Entry Type: OData V2
  • Request Entry Name:  /SAP/ESH_SEARCH_SRV/0001
  • Tenant ID: 000000000742874876

These three data formed a unique “Request Entry Point”.  When both Request Entry Type (= OData V2) and Tenant ID (= 000000000742874876) don’t change, each different entry name determines one unique “Request Entry Point”.  It helps to analyze the performance behavior of one specific program.

The Request Entry Type is the type of a request entering the system, for example, a Remote Function Call (RFC Call), or a Fiori App call (OData V2).  The Request Entry Name is the name of a request entering the system, like a program name.

 

Single Work Process Samples

Now we go to the details page of this “Request Entry Point” (see below). It shows ABAP CPU Time for this “Request Entry Point”.  Since we are only looking at one specific program, so the name of "Single Work Process".  On top of that, we might have multiple executions of the same program, so the name of "Samples".   Hence the chart shows the Single Work Process Samples by each program name listed according to a timeline (Collection Time Stamp).

The highest CPU consumption happens at 2024-09-20 04:10:00 with program /IWBEP/CL_MGW_ABS_DATA========CP. 

By clicking the program /IWBEP/CL_MGW_ABS_DATA========CP in the Legend, we can see four Single Work Process Samples collected at four different times. 

Four Single Work Process Samples collected at four different times for Program /IWBEP/CL_MGW_ABS_DATA========CP

By clicking the last entry, we got below Single Work Process Sample screen to show more data on this process in three tabs. 

  • The Work Process: Data tab provides details about the chosen work process sample, including information about the workload as well as session and logon information.
  • The Work Process: Metrics tab provides important metrics of the chosen work process sample, including response times, the ABAP memory consumption of a single work process, and the number of read and changed database records since the last sampling.
  • The ABAP Stack Trace tab goes directly to the code that was being executed when this work process sample was taken.   

 Response Time Info within Single Work Process Sample Screen

When clicking on ABAP Statistics Record button, we get to the ABAP Statistics Record screen the same as we approach from the System Workload app.

 ABAP Statistics Record screen

To summarize, we can follow the below flow to link all the data together: from Sampled Work Process Data to Request Entry Point to Single Work Process Sample to ABAP Statistics Record. 

Navigating from Sampled Work Process Data to Request Entry Point to Single Work Process Sample to ABAP Statistics Record

Not only the ABAP CPU Time, but we can also explore the Main Memory, Dialog Work Process Utilization and Background Work Process Utilization in a similar way. 

 

Conclusion

This blog discussed three important aspects of collecting/analyzing system workload data: the Server Response Time, the ABAP Statistics Records, the Sampled Work Process Data and the Single Work Process Sample.  The ABAP Statistics Records collects the information contributed by the completed workloads not collected in the previous collection point.  The data is collected every minute.  The Sampled Work Process Data show the information of one or many workloads running at one specific time.  It is collected every second. The Single Work Process Sample is the contributor to the Sampled Work Process Data for deep diving into a single work process sample.

 

References

  • SAP Help: Monitoring the System Workload
  • Blog: Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Clicking Through
  • Blog: Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – The Key Concepts 
  • Blog: Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Diving into Your Own System Workload (to be published)

entry.title: Recent Updates on Manifesto Eletrônico de Documentos Fiscais
entry.content:
Versão em português

Hi everyone,

The SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, outbound invoicing option for Brazil team has released updates on the Manifesto Eletrônico de Documentos Fiscais (MDF-e). The update complies with SEFAZ Technical Note 2024.002 1.01 Version. 

The new update changes the layout of the XML files, which now have the mapping of the following fields: indPrestacaoParcial, infNFePresParcial, and chNFe. You can implement these changes in the outbound invoicing option for Brazil by following the instructions on the SAP Note 3508360.

Take a look at the latest updates and features in our What's New session. And for a step-by-step guide on how to enable your system to submit MDF-e (Manifesto Eletrônico de Documentos Fiscais), refer to the following SAP Help Portal pages:

  • Integration > MDF-e Specifics
  • Using Outbound Nota Fiscal > MDF-e Specifics

Related blog posts:

  • How to Easily Browse the SAP Help Portal Pages for Outbound Nota Fiscal
  • An Introduction to SAP Document and Reporting Compliance: Navigating the SAP Help Portal

You can sign up for email alerts when new SAP Notes are released or updated in the components listed below; just follow the instructions in SAP Knowledge Base Article 2171560.

  • FI-LOC-NFE-BR-OUT
  • LOD-LH-NFE

Use the regulatory change manager tool to find out if a solution to a legal change is planned. Take a look at SAP Knowledge Base Article 3421995 and the regulatory change manager page to understand how to use this tool.

Have you found this blog post useful? If so, choose “like” (kudos), share it with your colleagues, and follow me for similar content.

You are welcome to leave your questions and read similar blog posts on the SAP Document and Reporting Compliance SAP Community. Don’t forget to follow the SAP Electronic Invoicing for Brazil (SAP Nota Fiscal Eletronica) tag and the SAP Document and Reporting Compliance community to keep up with the latest NF-e news.

Thank you, and until next time,
Thiago


Tags: brazil, brasil, einvoice, einvoicing, e-invoicing, electronic invoice, electronic invoicing, edocument, edoc, e-document, e-docs, e-doc, e-docs, drc, document compliance, nota fiscal eletrônica, electronic nota fiscal, outbound, nf-e, technical note, 2024.002, nt 2024.002, documento fiscal eletronico, electronic fiscal document

entry.title: MDF-e Nota Técnica 2024.002 Versão 1.01: Novos campos no Manifesto Eletrônico de Documentos Fiscais
entry.content:
English version here

Olá pessoal,

O time de SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, opção de emissão de fatura de saída para o Brasil entregou atualizações no Manifesto Eletrônico de Documentos Fiscais (MDF-e). A atualização está conforme a Nota Técnica 2024.002 Versão 1.01 da SEFAZ.

A nova atualização altera o layout dos arquivos XML, que agora têm o mapeamento dos seguintes campos: indPrestacaoParcial, infNFePresParcial e chNFe. Você pode implementar essas alterações na opção de faturamento de saída para o Brasil seguindo as instruções da SAP Note 3508360.

Confira as últimas atualizações e recursos na página Novidades do Nota fiscal de saída no portal SAP Help. E para um guia passo a passo sobre como habilitar seu sistema para enviar MDF-e (Manifesto Eletrônico de Documentos Fiscais), consulte as seguintes páginas do portal SAP Help:

  • Integração > Específicos de MDF-e
  • Utilizar o Nota fiscal de saída > Específicos de MDF-e

Blog posts relacionados:

  • Dashboard do Usage Analytics
  • Como navegar facilmente nas páginas do SAP Help Portal para Outbound Nota Fiscal
  • Navegando no portal SAP Help (inglês)

Você pode receber alerta por email quando novas SAP Notes forem lançadas ou atualizadas nos componentes listados abaixo; basta seguir as instruções do SAP Knowledge Base Article 2171560.

  • FI-LOC-NFE-BR-OUT
  • LOD-LH-NFE

Acesse o gerenciador de modificações legais para ter uma noção do que está planejado para ser suportado. Consulte o SAP Knowledge Base Article 3421995 e a página Regulatory Change Manager para entender como usar esta ferramenta.

Gostou desse post? Dê um “like” (kudos) e compartilhe o conteúdo com seus colegas. Sinta-se à vontade para deixar suas perguntas no SAP Community e ler posts semelhantes no blogging space do SAP Document and Reporting Compliance. Para acompanhar as últimas notícias da NF-e, siga a tag SAP Electronic Invoicing for Brazil (SAP Nota Fiscal Eletronica) e a comunidade SAP Document and Reporting Compliance.

Obrigado e até a próxima,
Thiago

Tags: brazil, brasil, einvoice, einvoicing, e-invoicing, electronic invoice, electronic invoicing, edocument, edoc, e-document, e-docs, e-doc, e-docs, drc, document compliance, nota fiscal eletrônica, electronic nota fiscal, outbound, nf3-e, nf-e, electric energy, energia elétrica, is-u, nota técnica 2024.002, 2024/002, 2024.00

entry.title: Two-tier ERP Master Data Replication Options
entry.content:
SAP two-tier ERP Integration

Integration is essential in two-tier scenarios. A comprehensive understanding of end-to-end business processes, including both master data and transactional data flows, is crucial for ensuring customer success. It's important to identify and address integration requirements early in the process. 

Data is broadly categorised as three areas. Master data, Transactional data, and Configuration data. Here in this blog we will be looking at master data replication options for two-tier ERP.

Master Data Replication

Master data is the core data that is absolutely essential for running operations within a business enterprise or unit. It is data about key business entities that provides context for business transactions and operations.

Master data replication between headquarters and subsidiaries is essential for following reasons:

  • Data Consistency: Ensures that both headquarters and subsidiaries have access to the same up-to-date information, minimising discrepancies and errors in data.
  • Operational Efficiency: Streamlines processes and operations by maintaining synchronized data, reducing the need for manual data entry and reconciliation.
  • Regulatory Compliance: Helps in maintaining compliance with regulatory requirements by ensuring that all parts of the organization adhere to the same standards and data practices.

Master Data Replication Option 1 - SAP Master Data Integration (MDI)

Customers with following cases should opt for MDI

  • Customers with Headquarter in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and multiple subsidiaries in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 
  • Customers utilises other SAP cloud solutions such SAP Ariba, SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Concur or non-SAP solutions that needed integration
  • Customers with requirement to harmonise master data across their system landscape
  • Customers who have SAP Business Technology Platform account

SAP Master Data Integration (MDI)

The SAP Master Data Integration service is a cloud service that enables you to handle distributed and federated sets of master data in a heterogenous landscape. Thus, SAP Master Data Integration is the central point for master data integration with the SAP Intelligent Suite.

The service uses SAP One Domain Model as the exchange format to achieve a consistent view of master data across end-to-end business processes in the integrated SAP Intelligent Suite.

SAP Master Data Integration provides reliable real-time replication in a hub-and-spoke approach. This allows every business application to have its own copy of the relevant master data.

 

                                                                                                       

MDI guiding principles

 Central Integration Point

MDI follows central integration point, often called a hub-and-spoke model, MDI acts as centralized system (hub) to manage data exchanges between multiple systems (spokes). Instead of connecting systems directly to each other which comes with centralised management, scalability, reduced complexity, and easy error handling.

One Domain Model

The SAP One Domain Model is an integrated data model that standardizes the representation of business objects across the SAP ecosystem. ODM ensures that these business objects are defined consistently across all SAP applications, including SAP S/4HANA, SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Ariba, and others.

ODM decouples from application specifics, provides well defined semantics for business objects.

 

Predefined Integration Flows

Predefined Integration Flows are ready-to-use, out-of-the-box integration scenarios designed to connect different systems efficiently. These flows are created based on best practices and include mappings, transformations, and communication settings, reducing the time and complexity involved in building custom integrations. 

Master Data Replication Option 2 – Data Replication Framework

Customers with following cases should opt for MDI

  • Customers with Headquarter in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and subsidiaries in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • Customers has requirement to integrate only S/4 solutions
  • Customers with simple landscape

Data Replication Framework

  • The Data Replication Framework (DRF) is a local business object change event processor that decides which business object instances will be replicated to defined target systems. 
  • The DRF is complemented by the key mapping framework and the value mapping framework to support non-harmonised identifiers and code lists.
  • DRF is hosted on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition which doesn’t require additional license
  • Applicable only for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

The options presented here are not the only approaches to data replication, but they represent the most relevant and prioritized solutions. 

For transactional data replication options refer the blog https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-sap/two-tier-erp-transactional-data-replication-options/ba-p/13879968

entry.title: Two-tier ERP with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Cloud Edition Overview
entry.content:
This blog aims to define two-tier ERP with SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition and provide details about pre-delivered content that supports different lines of business.

Two-tier ERP overview

Two-tier ERP addresses groups of companies including headquarters and subsidiaries. It comprises a headquarters ERP with high landscape complexity and being highly customized, and a subsidiaries ERP to support quick scale at lower costs with low landscape complexity. Both headquarters and subsidiaries ERP are integrated in a hybrid landscape with suitable implementation and process optimizations.

Two-tier ERP integration content covers connecting SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition as shown in the image below . Integration content included covering critical and many end-to-end scenarios and can be activated on-demand subjected business requirements. To know more click on " What is two-tier ERP? " page on sap.com


Two-Tier ERP Setup

Two-tier ERP integration between headquarters on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and subsidiaries running on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition might have different needs based on customer preferences

Two-Tier ERP Overview

Let us understand 3 different levels of integration in details

  1. Loosely coupled process – It can address requirements that can be executed in two-tier ERP set up without the real need of system-to-system integration connectivity. For example check the blog on Group Reporting  for more information 
  2. Master Data integration – It is very important to have master data integration specially product master and business partner in both the system . For example check the blog on Master Data Integration  for more information
  3. Tightly coupled process – It can address requirements  that can be executed in Two-tier ERP set up by leveraging SAP delivered communication scenario . For example sales office scenarios

From a functional scope perspective, SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition offers pre-delivered two-tier ERP integrations that cover finance, order-to-cash, and procurement needs of organizations, including those in Professional Services and Manufacturing. These integrations orchestrate end-to-end processes connectivity between connected systems.

To understand how requirements can be categorized into analytics, master data, and process capabilities in a Two-tier ERP with SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, let's consider the scope of order-to-cash-related requirements

The image above displays the relevant list of scope items available in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud that could address two-tier ERP order to cash related scope .

However, users can explore the given path to understand the full range of SAP-delivered two-tier ERP capabilities  SAP Best Practices for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and visit

accelerators > Implementation> Two-Tier ERP_End-to-End Business Processes for SAP S/4HANA Cloud 

You can also reach out two-tier ERP community for more clarifications

entry.title: Sales Office scenarios in Two-Tier ERP
entry.content:
Reflecting on the recently concluded Two-Tier ERP Confluence event, where we focused on integration and various sales office scenarios, this blog will bring a business perspective to the following key sales office scenarios and their significance:

  • Sale from Local Sales Office (Goods and Basic Services)
  • Sale from Central Stock, Direct Delivery to Customer from HQ
  • Returns Handling at Sales Office, with Credit and Debit Memo
  • Sale from HQ, Direct Procurement from Local Sales Office
  • Services from Sales Office for HQ
  • Installation, Warranty, Repair from Sales Office
  • Predictive Maintenance with AIN from Sales Office

Now, let’s take a deeper look at the different business scenarios, particularly focusing on sales from local offices.

A local sales office scenario is a prime example of loosely coupled scenario, which has been highlighted in the Two-Tier ERP Overview. In contrast, other sales office cases often involve dependent subsidiaries, which can be referred as tightly coupled scenario. You can find the relevant info here in the Two-Tier ERP Overview. Regardless of the structural differences between loosely coupled and tightly coupled, most of these scenarios can be grouped into two main categories:

  1. Inventory Sales and Returns Management
  2. Sale of services

Among the scenarios, listed above, the sale from the local sales office is well-known, but understanding why this scenario is crucial from a business perspective requires a deeper dive. This scenario enables regional sales offices to operate autonomously, driving localized decision-making, faster customer response times, and greater flexibility in meeting market demands. It is particularly beneficial when dealing with different compliance requirements, currency, or language variations across regions.

The Drop-Ship Scenario: An End-to-End Perspective

Let's now turn our attention to one of the most common sales processes from End-to-End perspective is “sale from central stock with direct delivery to the customer from headquarters”, commonly known as Drop-Ship scenario. This process offers significant advantages in maintaining lean inventories at sales offices while ensuring customer satisfaction with direct deliveries.

The following is an End-to-End scenario that offers detailed process information, outlining where each step is executed within a business context.

Future Exploration

In the upcoming blogs, we will take a deeper dive into each of the process steps outlined in the End-to-End scenario, exploring different variants and how they can benefit the business.

We will also take a deeper look in to Returns Management, in future blogs.

Sale of Services in a Two-Tier ERP Deployment

As outlined earlier, in Fig 1, the sale of services is another key scenario. For the sale of services, as outlined above, local subsidiaries manage both customer interaction and service delivery, allowing for greater agility and a better understanding of local needs. The Two-Tier ERP deployment model enables seamless integration between the local and HQ systems, ensuring smooth data flow across the organization.

For more information on warranty management, you can refer the blog here: https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-sap/warranty-claim-process-in-headquarter-subsidiary-model-for-two-tier-erp/ba-p/13743032

You can also reach out two-tier ERP community for more clarifications
